title: The ride of a lifetime
description: BUSINESS NEWS - I spent a few days over...
bodyText:
BUSINESS NEWS - I spent a few days over Christmas reading ‘The ride of a lifetime’ by Robert Iger. I’d highly recommend it.

Iger became CEO of the Walt Disney Company in 2005, which was a difficult time for the company. Competition was more intense than ever, and technology was changing faster than at any time in the company’s history.


> Iger uses the book to tell his story and reflects on the lessons he learned from starting at the bottom in 1974 to getting the top job in 2005. What really struck me was his ability to take large, calculated risks.


As he rose through the ranks of ABC, and then Disney (after Disney acquired ABC), the repeated pattern of his rise was his risk-taking and the payoffs.

You can’t win big if you don’t take big risks is a central message in the book.

There are numerous examples of the risks he took, but I want to write about the acquisition of Pixar Studios to illustrate the way he operated (and his boldness)


> Acquisition of Pixar


In 2005 Disney was in big trouble. Its share price had almost halved, and the animation division of Disney was a mess. Iger had just been made CEO and was charged with turning the company around.

The movies Disney was releasing at the time simply weren’t good enough (you may remember The Hunchback of Notre Dame, Hercules, Mulan, Tarzan, Fantasia 2000, Dinosaur, Atlantis, Treasure Planet – all flops at the box office).


> Iger recalls his thinking at the time – ‘As animations goes, so goes the company. Disney animation was the brand. And it was a mess’.


Iger attended his first board meeting as CEO in late 2005. The expectation was that he would lay out a plan for turning Disney around. He goes into detail on what happened -

Iger began the meeting by detailing the many failures of Disney’s animation division. He contrasts this with the success of Pixar Studios (Pixar Studios was Steve Jobs creation and a direct competitor of Disney animation at the time. You may remember Pixar’s early films Toy Story, Monsters Inc and Finding Nemo – all great successes.).

Iger then lays out 3 paths to the board for Disney Animation – sticking to current management (obviously out of the question), identify new talent to take over running the division, or buy Pixar Studios.

He writes ‘The response to the idea was so explosive that if I were holding a gavel I would have used it to bring the court to order’

The board were vehemently against it, but intrigued enough that they gave him the yellow light to explore it.


> Pixar was valued at $6 billion, with Steve Jobs owning half the shares. The chances of a purchase were slim. Jobs had a fractious relationship with Disney under their previous CEO, Michael Eisner. They weren’t on speaking terms following some very public disputes.


The board were also reluctant to give Iger approval to make such a large acquisition only a couple of months into his tenure.

Iger’s first step was to broach the idea with Steve Jobs. He recalls the phone call he made to Steve –

I spent the morning building up courage to make the call, and finally did so in the early afternoon. I didn’t reach him, which was a relief, but as I was driving home from the office at around six-thirty, he returned my call.

Iger: Hey, I have another crazy idea. Can I come see you in a day or two to discuss it?

Jobs: Tell me now (I didn’t appreciate just how much Steve liked radical ideas)

Iger: I’ve been thinking about our respective futures. What do you think of the idea of Disney buying Pixar?

I waited for him to hang up or erupt in laughter. The quiet before his response seemed endless.

Instead, he said ‘You know, that’s not the craziest idea in the world’


> In 2006, barely a year into Iger’s tenure, the Pixar deal closed, and Pixar became a part of Disney. This was the turning point for Disney.


Today, Disney is the largest and most respected media company in the world, counting Pixar, Marvel, Lucasfilm and 21st Century Fox among its properties. Its value increased nearly 5 times what it was when Iger took over, and he is recognized as one of the most innovative and successful CEOs of our era.

A central lesson of his book is that while nothing is a sure thing, you still need at the very least to be willing to take big risks. You can’t have big wins without them.

I like that message. It doesn’t mean be impulsive. Simply that you can't win big if you don’t take big risks.

Matthew Matthee

Matthew Matthee has a wealth management business that specialises in retirement planning and investments. He writes about financial markets, investments, and investor psychology. He holds a Masters Degree in Economics from Stellenbosch University and a Post Graduate Diploma in Financial Planning from UFS. [email protected]
